reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented union commitment to (a) assessment tools that do not function as performance audit creep on teachers; (b) workload neutrality in implementation; (c) transparency in SES-indicator construction to prevent covert gatekeeping by school staff.
reactions.interest: Organised teachers' labour interest in protecting assessment autonomy, workload, and working conditions; preventing assessment redesign from becoming a backdoor performance-management tool.
reactions.public_good_frame: Fair assessment design and transparent admission rules serve equity only if they do not shift hidden selection pressure onto teachers (e.g. via interview panels, reputation-based filtering) or increase marking/diagnostic burden without resource allocation.
reactions.argument: Assessment redesign for admission (replacing central exam) carries two hard constraints for union support: (1) the new assessment must be decoupled from any teacher performance-audit framework — if Oktatási Hivatal or Ministry later uses coaching-resistance data to grade teacher effectiveness, the union cannot endorse the redesign; (2) any shift to school-level selection channels (interviews, 'aptitude profiles') must be explicitly prohibited by regulation and monitored, because without this, quota reform simply migrates gatekeeping from exam-coaching to informal teacher discretion, increasing workload and creating reputational liability. The SES-indicator construction is a union concern only insofar as it should not require teachers to collect or certify family income data — that is administrative work and a privacy burden. If the redesign meets these conditions, union position is neutral-to-supportive on the equity goal, conditional on no performance-audit creep and workload neutrality.
reactions.condition_to_change: Union support becomes opposition if: (a) the new assessment data are used in teacher or school performance evaluation systems within five years; (b) admission decisions are shifted to school-level panels without explicit regulatory prohibition on informal SES-skewing criteria; (c) teachers are required to administer or score the new assessment without additional time allocation. Union support becomes full support if: (a) legislation explicitly decouples the redesigned assessment from teacher performance frameworks; (b) implementation includes funded training for schools to prevent interview-based gatekeeping; (c) the Ministry commits to central administration of the new assessment, not school-level delegation.

reactions.label: no_position
reactions.interest: Organised teachers' labour interest in sustainable employment, workload, and income security.
reactions.public_good_frame: Phased reduction of selective seats affects teacher employment and professional structure, but the union's interest in the outcome (equity gain or loss) is secondary to its interest in managing employment transitions.
reactions.argument: The phased phase-down of selective seats (S2) does not directly implicate wages, workload, or strike/union rights in the framing of this scenario. It affects school structure and pupil sorting, but the scenario does not address: (a) whether teachers in phased-down selective tracks retain employment in general schools; (b) whether salary scales differ between selective and general track; (c) whether consolidation triggers involuntary redeployment. Until these employment-transition conditions are specified, the union has no position on the structural reform itself. The union does have a position on any *implementation* that would be: no workforce reduction, salary protection during transition, and consultation on redeployment. These are not stated in S2, so we mark no_position here and defer to an employment-transition scenario.
reactions.condition_to_change: If S2 were paired with explicit employment guarantees (e.g. 'teachers in phased-down selective tracks retain salary in general-school posts') or redeployment terms, the union would move to conditional support. If S2 entails involuntary reduction or redeployment without consultation, the union moves to opposition on the grounds of labour security, not equity.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented union position that structural reform (comprehensive schooling) is compatible with union interests only if paired with: (a) binding commitments to teacher supply and training; (b) workload safeguards against uncompensated differentiation; (c) statutory class-size caps; (d) explicit decoupling from performance-audit frameworks.
reactions.interest: Organised teachers' labour interest in: workload stability, professional autonomy in classroom differentiation (not audit-driven), adequate training, and job security across the transition.
reactions.public_good_frame: Comprehensive schooling to age 14 can serve equity goals, but only if accompanied by teacher supply, professional investment, and working-condition standards that make differentiated instruction feasible without degrading teaching conditions or forcing teachers to absorb the cost of structural change.
reactions.argument: S3 is the highest-stakes structural scenario for teachers. The union's conditional support rests on recognising that delayed selection to age 14 aligns with documented union values (equity, reduced early sorting) *and* creates a feasibility constraint: differentiated instruction at scale requires more teaching preparation time, smaller classes, and diagnostic/remedial coordination infrastructure that Hungary currently lacks. The scenario notes that 'teacher supply is a binding constraint' and that 'teacher shortage makes redistribution... infeasible in the short term, degrading academic quality.' The union cannot endorse S3 unless the implementation commitment includes: (1) statutory reduction in class sizes for comprehensive upper grades (max 20 pupils in mixed-ability groups), funded and legislated; (2) a binding 5-year teacher-supply plan with salary incentives and retraining stipends; (3) explicit prohibition on using differentiation data for teacher performance audit (this is critical — if Oktatási Hivatal later uses remedial-tracking data to grade teacher effectiveness, the union will oppose S3 retroactively); (4) transition funding for diagnostic/remedial support *before* structural rollout, not retroactively. If these conditions are absent, S3 becomes a unfunded mandate that pushes unpaid work onto teachers, and the union must oppose on labour grounds despite equity alignment.
reactions.condition_to_change: Union moves to full support if implementation includes: (a) legislated class-size caps (max 20 for mixed-ability comprehensive grades); (b) binding multi-year teacher-salary floor tied to CPI + 3% to enable recruitment; (c) 200+ hours/year statutory preparation time for differentiated-instruction planning; (d) diagnostic/remedial funding routed to schools before structural transition. Union remains conditional if one of these is missing (e.g. class-size cap only, but no salary floor). Union moves to opposition if: (a) the reform is legislated without teacher-supply guarantees; (b) differentiation data are used in teacher evaluation frameworks; (c) the transition timeline is accelerated below 5 years without additional resource commitment.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented union critique that non-structural 'compensation' frameworks (TEIP-style targeted funding without structural reform) function as permanent entrenchment of sorting, shifting responsibility for equity to teachers in disadvantaged schools without addressing root causes. Union values professional autonomy and structural fairness; S4 violates both by leaving sorting intact and burdening disadvantaged-school teachers with uncompensated remedial work.
reactions.interest: Organised teachers' labour interest in equitable professional conditions, structural fairness (not two-tier systems), and protection against being made responsible for offsetting systemic sorting via unpaid effort.
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation-without-reform entrenches sorting and creates a two-tier teaching profession, where disadvantaged-school teachers are implicitly tasked with offsetting the damage of early selection without structural change. This is both inequitable to students (root cause unaddressed) and exploitative of teachers (expects remedial work without corresponding investment or recognition).
reactions.argument: S4 is fundamentally incompatible with union labour and professional interests, for two reasons: (1) The scenario explicitly states that 'no comparator has demonstrated compensation offsetting selection as early as age 10' and 'leaves the sorting mechanism itself intact.' The union's position is that this is precisely the wrong policy framing: if sorting is permitted to stand, compensation becomes a permanent burden on disadvantaged-school teachers to manage its effects, without ever removing the source. This is unacceptable. (2) TEIP-style targeted funding in Portugal did not reduce selection age and did not face Hungary's teacher shortage; the scenario's own political-risk field notes that 'targeted funding is the first line cut in fiscal consolidation,' which means S4 is structurally fragile and teaches teachers that their additional workload (remedial support, diagnostics) is contingent on year-to-year budget survival. The union cannot endorse a framework that institutionalises lower teaching standards and higher workload for some teachers on a permanent, underfunded basis. The scenario notes that 'teacher shortage undermines curriculum-standard and support delivery in disadvantaged schools' — this is precisely the crisis S4 creates: structurally sorting pupils, chronically underfunding remedial support, and then expecting teachers to absorb the gap. This violates union values on professional dignity and structural fairness.
reactions.condition_to_change: Union moves from opposition to conditional support only if S4 is paired with: (a) legislation abolishing selective entry by age 12 (i.e. structural reform is bundled with compensation, not replacement); (b) binding statutory commitment to disadvantaged-school funding at 150% of advantaged-school per-pupil allocation, with protection against budget cuts; (c) class-size caps and workload guarantees in disadvantaged schools, not merely funding allocation. Without structural reform, opposition stands. If S4 is presented as a temporary transition toward later structural reform (e.g. 'TEIP funding for 3 years, then S3 is legislated'), the union would move to conditional-support position contingent on the structural reform timeline being legislated *before* compensation funding is allocated.
